target_name: Claude Design vs Figma Make vs Lovable vs Vercel v0 vs Codex vs Cursor vs Claude Code
summary: A current-source AI resilience comparison of seven AI-assisted creation-to-published UI/product workflows, now scored as one cohort. Cursor leads at 59/75 because it sits deepest in the editor, repository, terminal, and review loop. Claude Code and Codex follow at 58/75 and 57/75 because they are similarly close to production code, with slightly different distribution and workflow-continuity profiles. Vercel v0 leads the visual/app-builder products at 54/75 through Vercel deploy, GitHub, preview, and app-building linkage. Figma Make scores 50/75 from design-system context and Figma distribution. Lovable scores 46/75 as a credible full-stack app builder with cost and assurance caveats. Claude Design scores 44/75 because it has impressive visual breadth but launch-stage operational evidence.

placement.Cursor: Upper-right: strongest same-cohort workflow control, with designer-ergonomics caveat
placement.Claude Code: Upper-right: terminal-native production control, with non-visual surface caveat
placement.Codex: Upper-right: strong agentic coding command center, slightly lower continuity than editor-native Cursor
placement.Vercel v0: Upper-right: best visual app-builder link to deploy and production surfaces
placement.Figma Make: Right of center: strongest design-context surface, below code-agent production control
placement.Lovable: Near center-right: app-builder depth, below stronger incumbent/code-agent control
placement.Claude Design: Right of center on output breadth, below workflow midpoint

quadrant.marker_convention: All seven targets are scored in the same 75-point cohort. Circles mark visual/app-builder surfaces; diamonds mark coding tools retained as a caveat about workflow entry point.
quadrant.rationale: The x-axis captures owned context, trust, distribution, and workflow state. The y-axis captures closeness to production control. Coding tools plot high because they work directly in repositories, editors, terminals, and review loops. Visual/app-builder tools plot lower when they stop at prototypes or first deploys without durable production-operation control.

context.rationale: Cursor indexes codebases, uses editor state, rules, terminal context, and PR history, giving it the deepest local implementation context in this cohort.

trust.rationale: Cursor publishes security, SOC 2 Type II, privacy-mode, and enterprise controls, while its own security page still cautions sensitive environments to assess risk.

distribution.rationale: The product benefits from AI-editor adoption and VS Code familiarity; recent reviewer transcripts also frame it as a default agentic coding workspace for many builders.

judgment.rationale: Cursor improves implementation throughput but does not own product judgment, brand taste, accessibility acceptance, or release decisions.

liability_governance.rationale: Review diffs, approval flows, security controls, privacy modes, and enterprise settings help governance, but customers remain accountable for shipped code.

frequency.rationale: Reviewer transcripts show frequent agentic coding use; for designers specifically, frequency depends on comfort working in a code editor.

operational_dependence.rationale: Cursor can sit inside the implementation loop through files, terminal commands, code review, agent tools, and codebase indexing.

system_position.rationale: It occupies the editor/agent layer where UI code is inspected, modified, run, reviewed, and committed.

switching_cost.rationale: Rules, indexed context, editor habits, and project setup create meaningful friction, though code remains portable.

budget_durability.rationale: Paid team and enterprise packaging ties spend to daily software delivery rather than one-off prototype generation.

model_improvement_test.rationale: Better models should increase value because Cursor attaches model output to repository context, terminal execution, and review loops.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: The codebase/editor position lowers wrapper risk, though Cursor still depends on access to frontier models and competing agentic editors.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Cursor documents agent tools for search, edit, terminal execution, MCP, and codebase search.

accountability_test.rationale: It can surface diffs and run checks, but production correctness, security, accessibility, and user acceptance remain human-owned.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: Transcripts show full-feature coding workflows and PR-oriented review, but not end-to-end responsibility for product outcomes.

context.rationale: Claude Code reads project files, executes commands, uses memory/rules, connects via MCP, and can integrate external context such as Figma through tools.

trust.rationale: Anthropic trust, enterprise packaging, and Claude distribution support buyer trust, though code-agent risk still requires customer controls.

distribution.rationale: Claude Code inherits Claude/Anthropic distribution and has terminal, IDE, and GitHub workflow surfaces.

judgment.rationale: It can reason through code and edge cases, but product judgment, UX quality, and release approval remain user responsibilities.

liability_governance.rationale: GitHub Actions, MCP controls, permissions, and review workflows help governance but do not transfer accountability.

frequency.rationale: Recent practitioner transcripts show recurring use for planning, coding, testing, and codebase exploration.

operational_dependence.rationale: It can modify files, run commands, create commits, and operate in real repos, making it part of the implementation loop.

system_position.rationale: Claude Code sits directly in terminal/IDE/GitHub workflows where production code changes happen.

switching_cost.rationale: Project memories, commands, MCP setup, and workflow habits create friction, although source code remains portable.

budget_durability.rationale: Claude paid plans and enterprise packaging make spend attach to software delivery, despite transcript-reported credit/cost management needs.

model_improvement_test.rationale: Stronger reasoning and coding models should directly improve repo-level planning, edits, testing, and debugging.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: Terminal, GitHub, MCP, and workflow integration reduce wrapper risk, but competition among code agents remains intense.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Claude Code is explicitly an agentic coding tool with MCP, IDE integrations, and GitHub workflows.

accountability_test.rationale: It can run checks and support reviews, but users still own correctness, security, accessibility, and production outcomes.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: Transcripts show sophisticated codebase work and production-style review, but not durable ownership after deployment.

context.rationale: Codex connects to repositories, cloud worktrees, IDE/terminal surfaces, skills, and PR review flows, but is less continuously editor-native than Cursor.

trust.rationale: OpenAI security, enterprise data controls, and Codex product positioning support trust for teams.

distribution.rationale: Codex benefits from ChatGPT/OpenAI distribution, app, IDE, and terminal surfaces.

judgment.rationale: It can reason through implementation, but product/design judgment and final release decisions remain external.

liability_governance.rationale: PR review, worktrees, comments, and business security controls support governance without assuming outcome liability.

frequency.rationale: Official and reviewer transcripts position Codex for recurring engineering tasks, review, refactors, and multi-agent work.

operational_dependence.rationale: Codex can modify repos, run work in cloud environments, support PRs, and integrate with local workflows.

system_position.rationale: It sits close to code, review, branch/worktree, terminal, and PR workflows.

switching_cost.rationale: Skills, automations, and OpenAI account workflows add friction, but code and repos remain portable.

budget_durability.rationale: Team credits, ChatGPT/OpenAI packaging, and engineering workflow value make budget durability stronger than prototype-only tools.

model_improvement_test.rationale: OpenAI coding-model improvements should directly increase Codex value across implementation, review, and multi-agent workflows.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: Repo, worktree, PR, skills, and automations reduce thin-wrapper risk, though the code-agent category is highly competitive.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Codex is positioned as a multi-agent coding command center with cloud environments, skills, automations, IDE, and terminal surfaces.

accountability_test.rationale: Codex can run checks and support review, but customers own acceptance and production consequences.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: Official evidence shows feature, refactor, migration, review, and app-building workflows, but not customer outcome ownership.

context.rationale: v0 can use repos, environment variables, Vercel configuration, design mode, databases, and APIs, but customer/product context often lives outside v0.

trust.rationale: Vercel security, trust, deployment protections, and enterprise controls support buyer trust.

distribution.rationale: v0 benefits from Vercel distribution, templates, one-click deploy, and developer-channel reach.

judgment.rationale: v0 can generate and revise apps, but product judgment, architecture, security, and release decisions still need human review.

liability_governance.rationale: GitHub, PRs, deploy protection, access controls, and Vercel platform defaults strengthen governance.

frequency.rationale: App and website iteration can be recurring, but v0 may not be a daily operating surface for every team.

operational_dependence.rationale: Repo import, deploy, preview, branch, and PR workflows can become part of shipping work.

system_position.rationale: v0 links AI generation to code, GitHub, Vercel deployment, and production preview workflows.

switching_cost.rationale: Git portability lowers lock-in while Vercel setup, deploy habits, and generated project context create some friction.

budget_durability.rationale: Team/business packaging and production software use support budget durability.

model_improvement_test.rationale: Better models should increase v0 value because outputs are tied to code, deploy, debugging, and platform workflows.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: Vercel linkage reduces wrapper risk, but visible prompt-to-app generation remains exposed to coding agents and model-native builders.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: v0 positions itself around agentic app/website building, planning, integrations, GitHub, and deployment.

accountability_test.rationale: v0 can route work through code and deployment controls, but users still own production correctness.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: v0 can create, publish, and deploy working applications, but durable operations still depend on customer process.

context.rationale: Figma Make can attach Figma designs, components, library styling, images, and team templates to prompt-to-app workflows.

trust.rationale: Figma paid plans, enterprise posture, collaboration, permissions, and security docs support buyer trust.

distribution.rationale: Make benefits from Figma files, collaboration graph, paid seats, Community, and designer mindshare.

judgment.rationale: Make accelerates prototypes and web apps but does not own product judgment, usability validation, or production acceptance.

liability_governance.rationale: Figma sharing, plan controls, and governance help, but Figma docs still place content rights and publishing responsibility on users.

frequency.rationale: Transcripts support repeated MVP/prototype use, but not daily production operation for most teams.

operational_dependence.rationale: Make supports previews, collaboration, code editing, backends, and publishing, but teams can still move implementation elsewhere.

system_position.rationale: It sits inside Figma where design files, components, previews, and team collaboration already happen.

switching_cost.rationale: Generated artifacts are useful, but teams can recreate outputs elsewhere if Figma context is not central.

budget_durability.rationale: Paid-seat and AI-credit packaging creates a budget path tied to existing Figma use.

model_improvement_test.rationale: Better models should improve Make because Figma owns design context, components, and collaboration surfaces.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: Figma context reduces wrapper risk, but prompt-to-app generation is exposed to coding agents and app builders.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Figma documents code, packages, publishing, and MCP/code-agent handoff for Make files.

accountability_test.rationale: Make can publish and share, but it does not absorb responsibility for production correctness, rights, compliance, or app outcomes.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: Transcripts and docs support strong interactive prototype/MVP outcomes, though not sustained production operation.

context.rationale: Lovable stores project code, chat context, GitHub sync, connectors, and app-building history, but deeper customer data usually lives elsewhere.

trust.rationale: Security scans, SSO, audit logs, publishing controls, and enterprise features improve trust, but production assurance remains customer-owned.

distribution.rationale: Lovable has direct app-builder distribution, templates, pricing tiers, connectors, and GitHub workflow without Figma/Vercel-scale incumbent channels.

judgment.rationale: Lovable can assemble apps quickly, but product, architecture, UX, and security judgment remain substantially user-owned.

liability_governance.rationale: Security and audit features help governance, but docs and transcript context reinforce that users must validate critical behavior.

frequency.rationale: Transcripts support recurring prototype and app-building use, but not universal daily operational dependence.

operational_dependence.rationale: Publishing, hosting, GitHub sync, cloud, and connectors make it operationally useful, though users can continue elsewhere.

system_position.rationale: Lovable spans generation, app code, publishing, GitHub, cloud/backend paths, and connectors.

switching_cost.rationale: GitHub sync/export improves portability while project history, connectors, and cloud setup create moderate friction.

budget_durability.rationale: Docs and transcripts show credit/usage-cost sensitivity, making large sustained production budgets less certain.

model_improvement_test.rationale: Better models should improve app assembly and debugging but also lower barriers for competing app builders.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: Lovable has more workflow depth than a thin wrapper, but prompt-to-app generation remains crowded and model-sensitive.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Agent mode, browser testing, connectors, GitHub sync, and cloud integration make it meaningfully agent-ready.

accountability_test.rationale: Security scans and controls help, but Lovable does not take responsibility for production correctness or app-specific assurance.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: Transcripts and docs support live full-stack app outcomes, with review and reliability caveats.

context.rationale: Claude Design can build design systems from codebases, design files, brand assets, and web capture, but production telemetry and customer state remain outside the product.

trust.rationale: It inherits Claude/Anthropic trust and paid-plan distribution, though product-specific governance evidence is still sparse.

distribution.rationale: Claude Design launches inside Claude for paid subscriber tiers and benefits from Claude distribution.

judgment.rationale: It expands visual exploration but does not own brand approval, design judgment, accessibility, or production acceptance.

liability_governance.rationale: Admin controls and org sharing exist, but launch-stage docs leave gaps around auditability, data residency, API maturity, and production governance.

frequency.rationale: Transcripts support repeated artifact/prototype use, but frequency varies by role and launch-stage limits.

operational_dependence.rationale: Evidence supports ideation, refinement, export, and handoff rather than dependence as a system of record or production control plane.

system_position.rationale: Claude Design can hand off to Claude Code and read design/code context, but does not itself own Git, deploy, or release workflows.

switching_cost.rationale: Outputs can be exported or recreated, and durable lock-in beyond Claude preference is not yet proven.

budget_durability.rationale: Paid-plan access and extra usage create a budget path, while transcript evidence flags limits and cost friction.

model_improvement_test.rationale: The product is model-native and powered by Anthropic multimodal capabilities, so model improvements should raise output breadth and quality.

wrapper_risk_test.rationale: The launch surface is easy to imitate unless design-system, collaboration, handoff, and workflow controls deepen.

agent_readiness_test.rationale: Claude Design packages handoff bundles for Claude Code and supports code-powered prototypes, but external integration evidence is early.

accountability_test.rationale: Customers still own correctness, rights, approval, brand fit, accessibility, and production use.

outcome_depth_test.rationale: It spans prototypes, slides, decks, one-pagers, collateral, and interactive visual artifacts, though not production operation.

Cursor: Deep repo/editor context, codebase indexing, terminal/tool execution, reviewable diffs, enterprise security posture, and strong daily workflow fit.
Claude Code: Strong terminal/IDE/GitHub positioning, MCP extensibility, project memory, real-file edits, and credible practitioner workflow evidence.
Codex: OpenAI distribution, repository/worktree/PR workflows, skills and automations, multi-agent command-center positioning, and strong model-improvement leverage.
Vercel v0: Best visual-builder bridge into code, GitHub, previews, Vercel deploys, and production sharing.
Figma Make: Design-file context, components, libraries, collaboration, stakeholder buy-in workflows, publishing, and agent handoff.
Lovable: Fast app generation, live publishing, GitHub sync, cloud/backend path, connectors, and increasingly explicit security tooling.
Claude Design: Chat-native visual creation, broad artifact range, design-system setup, inline refinement, export/handoff, and Claude distribution.

Cursor: Not a visual design surface; designers need code fluency or engineering partnership, and source-code privacy/security concerns remain material.
Claude Code: Requires technical workflow comfort, can consume credits quickly, and is not optimized as a visual design review surface.
Codex: Not a visual design tool; switching costs are moderate and production acceptance still needs human review.
Vercel v0: Some moat belongs to Vercel rather than v0 alone; output quality, security, and architecture still require review.
Figma Make: Still more design/prototype surface than production operating system; accountability remains user-owned.
Lovable: Credit/usage costs, production assurance, and security review remain exposed; less incumbent distribution than Figma or Vercel.
Claude Design: Research-preview launch, sparse operational governance, low switching cost, and transcript-reported generic output/bug/limit concerns.

Cursor: Codebase index, editor state, rules, terminal execution, PR/review loop, and daily developer workflow position.
Claude Code: Terminal and GitHub workflow embed, MCP ecosystem, project memory, Anthropic distribution, and handoff compatibility with Claude Design.
Codex: OpenAI account distribution, coding models, cloud worktrees, PR/review loop, skills, automations, and multi-surface access.
Vercel v0: Vercel deployment surface, GitHub/PR path, preview links, enterprise security, and developer distribution.
Figma Make: Figma design context, components/libraries, collaboration graph, existing paid-seat distribution, and design-to-prototype continuity.
Lovable: Project/chat/code context, publish path, GitHub sync, cloud/backend workflows, connectors, and security scans.
Claude Design: Claude multimodal model surface, Claude distribution, broad visual artifact range, design-system setup, and Claude Code handoff.

commoditizing_layers: Basic code edits without review context

Cursor: Run designer-led UI-generation tests, measure design-system adherence, and validate security/privacy settings for enterprise codebases.
Claude Code: Score with a controlled designer-to-UI implementation run and test review discipline, rollback, accessibility, and security behavior.
Codex: Run the same UI task protocol as visual builders and separate app-generation speed from reviewed production-readiness.
Vercel v0: Run controlled live tasks across existing repos and measure bug rate, review quality, deployment handoff, and post-launch maintenance.
Figma Make: Prove production handoff, lifecycle governance, and sustained usefulness after engineering takes over.
Lovable: Reduce cost friction, prove sustained maintenance workflows, and make production security guarantees more operationally concrete.
Claude Design: Publish clearer governance/API/integration docs and run side-by-side creation-to-publish tasks against the other cohort members.

final_verdict: The same-cohort rerun moves the coding tools from contextual diamonds into the ranked set. Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex lead because they operate closest to production code, test/build loops, and reviewable changes. That does not mean they are better design products; it means the AI resilience rubric rewards owned workflow context and production control. v0 is the strongest visual/app-builder product because Vercel gives it deploy and GitHub linkage. Figma Make remains the strongest design-context surface. Lovable is a credible full-stack app builder but must prove trust, cost durability, and sustained maintenance. Claude Design remains high-upside but launch-stage: broad artifact generation and Claude Code handoff are promising, but governance, integrations, and production-operation evidence are still thin.

confidence.reason: Official evidence is current and YouTube transcript evidence covers every product, but controlled live UI-generation runs were not performed. Confidence is highest for code-agent workflow placement and lower for designer-specific usability of code-native tools.

by_target.Cursor: Medium-high. Official security/docs evidence is strong and transcript evidence is recent, but UI-generation-for-designers needs controlled runs.
by_target.Claude Code: Medium-high. Official Claude Code docs and current transcripts support the score; designer-specific usability remains less proven.
by_target.Codex: Medium-high. OpenAI official evidence is strong and transcripts cover comparative use; design-specific UI runs are still missing.
by_target.Vercel v0: Medium. Official evidence is strong and transcripts add context, but no controlled live task run was performed.
by_target.Figma Make: Medium-high. Official docs and transcripts align on design-context strengths; production depth is still less proven.
by_target.Lovable: Medium. Official docs and transcripts support a credible app-builder workflow, with cost/reliability caveats.
by_target.Claude Design: Low-medium. Official launch docs and transcripts are current, but the product is new and operational evidence is sparse.

unknowns_that_would_change_the_score_mos: Controlled same-prompt UI-generation runs for all seven tools
Enterprise governance and production approval evidence

evidence_notes.shared_evidence_window: Most recent 12 months for change-sensitive evidence, with current official product, docs, pricing, integration, trust/security, launch materials, and April 23, 2026 transcript pulls prioritized.

sources_by_function.supports: Defines the product surface, user, workflow, supported outputs, and scoped target boundary for each cohort member.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: Highest: current official product pages, launch posts, help centers, docs, and pricing pages.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Use current official pages at report time; do not reuse older repo summaries for product facts.

sources_by_function.supports: Substantiates design-system context, code/Git handoff, publishing, deployment, repository sync, terminal/editor workflows, and MCP/tool integrations.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: High: official developer docs, integration docs, and product docs; supplement with transcript evidence for user-visible workflow friction.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Prefer current docs and fresh transcript pulls; avoid dated repo task evidence for this rerun.

sources_by_function.supports: Informs distribution, budget durability, paid-plan fit, usage-limit friction, and team/enterprise packaging.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: Medium-high: official pricing and packaging pages plus recent transcript context for cost/friction patterns.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Use latest pricing pages and label transcript cost comments as qualitative, not official pricing facts.

sources_by_function.supports: Checks incident history, service disruption evidence, and whether the product can be operationally depended on.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: High for official status pages; medium for transcript-reported bugs/friction.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Use current status pages and current transcripts; do not treat old incidents as current unless repeated.

sources_by_function.supports: Supports trust, liability governance, admin control, auditability, source-code privacy, data handling, and enterprise readiness.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: Highest: official trust centers, security docs, admin guides, and governance docs.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Use current trust/security docs and mark missing or launch-stage controls as uncertainty.

sources_by_function.supports: Captures hands-on workflow friction, output quality, cost/credit sensitivity, designer/developer ergonomics, and reviewer uncertainty for every target.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: Medium: third-party qualitative evidence; official demo transcripts are used only for workflow demonstration, not independent validation.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Use transcript pull date and video dates; cross-check product facts against official sources.

sources_by_function.supports: Explains why Codex, Cursor, and Claude Code are scored in the same cohort but retained as diamond markers: they are codebase-native UI-generation tools, not visual-first design surfaces.
sources_by_function.evidence_weight: Analytical normalization using the same rubric, official docs, and transcript context.
sources_by_function.stale_context_handling: Treat marker shape as a caveat, not a scoring exemption; refresh with controlled live UI-generation runs.

evidence_notes.youtube_transcript_method: YouTube review/tutorial/demo searches were run for all seven products. Captions were pulled with yt-dlp on April 23, 2026 and used as qualitative third-party context, not official product facts or controlled empirical task evidence.

evidence_notes.youtube_transcript_read: Coding-tool transcripts support strong production-control placement but caveat technical fluency, cost, and review burden. Visual/app-builder transcripts support speed and accessibility while reinforcing design sameness, credit/cost, launch-stage, and production-readiness concerns.

comparison.relative_summary: When scored as one cohort, coding tools lead because the rubric rewards proximity to implementation context, versioned code, terminal/test loops, and production review. That is a category effect as much as an execution effect: Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex are not visual-first design products, so their diamond marker remains as a caveat. v0 is the strongest app-builder because it bridges AI generation to Vercel deploys and GitHub. Figma Make has the strongest design-context story. Lovable is a credible app-builder but weaker on trust and cost durability. Claude Design is high-upside but early and less operationally embedded.
key_differences: Cursor: strongest editor/repo context and daily workflow embed, but less visual/designer-native.
Claude Code: strongest terminal/Claude ecosystem handoff story, but requires technical workflow fluency.
Codex: strongest OpenAI multi-agent command-center distribution, but not a visual design surface.
Vercel v0: best visual app-builder connection to deploy, previews, GitHub, and production sharing.
Figma Make: best design-system and collaboration context, but weaker production control.
Lovable: strong app-builder and deploy workflow, but trust, cost, and assurance remain exposed.
Claude Design: broad visual artifact generation and Claude Code handoff, but sparse launch-stage governance and operational proof.
comparison.category_vs_execution_effects: The coding tools lead largely because their category is closer to production code. Their diamond shape is a caveat that they are scored in the same cohort but serve a different entry point: code editor, terminal, and repo rather than a visual workspace. Among visual/app-builder tools, v0 leads on production linkage, Figma Make leads on design context, Lovable leads on full-stack app-builder accessibility, and Claude Design leads on broad visual artifact breadth but trails on workflow embed.
